## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena
CRITICAL: Before starting to work on a coding task, call the `initial_instructions` tool to read the 'Serena Instructions Manual'.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits détectés, langue détectée, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- typescript-standards:enforce-ts-standards: Enforce type-safe TypeScript patterns with Zod validation, pattern matching, and discriminated unions to prevent runtime errors. Use when writing TypeScript code, reviewing for type safety, designing data validation flows, or refactoring to eliminate impossible states.
- oss-security-audit:audit-oss-package: Security audit of a third-party package (npm, pip, homebrew, cargo, gem…) before
using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the
package manifest.
Use when the user says "is this package safe", "audit this dependency",
"check this npm/pip/brew package", "can I trust this library", or asks
whether a package is trustworthy before adding it to a project.

import Foundation

/// Centralised UserDefaults key names, prefixed to avoid collisions with
/// other subsystems that might share the same defaults suite.
public enum AppPreferenceKeys: String {
    case refreshIntervalSeconds = "ai-tracker.refreshIntervalSeconds"
    case launchAtLogin = "ai-tracker.launchAtLogin"
    case logLevel = "ai-tracker.logLevel"
    case menuBarSegments = "ai-tracker.menuBarSegments"
    case menuBarSegmentsInitialized = "ai-tracker.menuBarSegmentsInitialized"
    case menuBarSeparator = "ai-tracker.menuBarSeparator"
    case chartConfigurations = "ai-tracker.chartConfigurations"
    case chartConfigurationsInitialized = "ai-tracker.chartConfigurationsInitialized"
    case ignoredAccounts = "ai-tracker.ignoredAccounts"
}

1	import AppKit
2	import SwiftUI
3	import AIUsagesTrackersLib
4	import AppIconKit
5	
6	@MainActor
7	final class AppDelegate: NSObject, NSApplicationDelegate {
8	    private var poller: UsagePoller?
9	    private var snapshotScheduler: SnapshotScheduler?
10	    private var accountMonitor: ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor?
11	    private var codexMonitor: CodexActiveAccountMonitor?
12	    private var pidGuard: AppPidGuard?
13	    private var usageStore: UsageStore?
14	    private var refreshState: RefreshState?
15	    private var statusItem: NSStatusItem?
16	    private var popover: NSPopover?
17	    private var appearanceObserver: NSKeyValueObservation?
18	    private var settingsWindowObserver: NSObjectProtocol?
19	
20	    /// Shared preferences store — exposed as static so the SwiftUI Settings scene
21	    /// (constructed before applicationDidFinishLaunching) can access it.
22	    static let sharedPreferences: UserDefaultsAppPreferences = UserDefaultsAppPreferences()
23	
24	    /// Shared usage store — populated in applicationDidFinishLaunching. The Settings
25	    /// scene is instantiated before the store exists, so it must read through this
26	    /// mutable holder. Nil until the first launch callback has run.
27	    static var sharedStore: UsageStore?
28	
29	    func applicationDidFinishLaunching(_ notification: Notification) {
30	        NSApplication.shared.setActivationPolicy(.accessory)
31	
32	        // No .app bundle ships with this SwiftPM executable, so AppKit has no
33	        // static icon slot — set the Dock / Cmd+Tab icon programmatically.
34	        if let icon = AppIconRenderer.makeImage(pixelSize: 1024) {
35	            NSApplication.shared.applicationIconImage = icon
36	        }
37	
38	        let home = FileManager.default.homeDirectoryForCurrentUser.path
39	        let guard_ = AppPidGuard(cacheDir: "\(home)/.cache/ai-usages-tracker")
40	        do {
41	            try guard_.acquire()
42	        } catch AppPidGuardError.alreadyRunning(let pid, _) {
43	            let alert = NSAlert()
44	            alert.messageText = "AI Usages Tracker is already running"
45	            alert.informativeText = "Another instance is active (PID \(pid)). Use the menu bar icon to quit it before launching a new one."
46	            alert.runModal()
47	            NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
48	            return
49	        } catch {
50	            let alert = NSAlert()
51	            alert.messageText = "Failed to start AI Usages Tracker"
52	            alert.informativeText = error.localizedDescription
53	            alert.runModal()
54	            NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
55	            return
56	        }
57	        pidGuard = guard_
58	
59	        Loggers.setPreferences(Self.sharedPreferences)
60	
61	        MenuBarSegmentsSeeder.seedIfNeeded(preferences: Self.sharedPreferences)
62	        ChartConfigurationsSeeder.seedIfNeeded(preferences: Self.sharedPreferences)
63	
64	        // Reconcile launch-at-login preference with system state — the user may
65	        // have disabled the entry in System Settings > Login Items directly.
66	        let launchService = LaunchAtLoginService.shared
67	        if Self.sharedPreferences.launchAtLogin != launchService.isEnabled {
68	            Self.sharedPreferences.launchAtLogin = launchService.isEnabled
69	        }
70	
71	        let fileManager = UsagesFileManager.shared
72	        let refreshState = RefreshState()
73	        let codexConnector = CodexConnector()
74	        let poller = UsagePoller(
75	            connectors: [ClaudeCodeConnector(), codexConnector],
76	            statusConnectors: [ClaudeStatusConnector(), CodexStatusConnector()],
77	            fileManager: fileManager,
78	            refreshState: refreshState,
79	            preferences: Self.sharedPreferences
80	        )
81	        let accountMonitor = ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor(
82	            fileManager: fileManager,
83	            onActiveAccountChanged: { [weak poller] _ in
84	                await poller?.pollOnce(force: true)
85	            }
86	        )
87	        let codexMonitor = CodexActiveAccountMonitor(
88	            onActiveAccountChanged: { [weak codexConnector, weak poller] _ in
89	                await codexConnector?.invalidateEmailCache()
90	                await poller?.pollOnce(force: true)
91	            }
92	        )
93	        let snapshotRecorder = SnapshotRecorder()
94	        let historyReader = UsageHistoryReader(rootPath: snapshotRecorder.rootPath)
95	        let snapshotScheduler = SnapshotScheduler(
96	            fileManager: fileManager,
97	            recorder: snapshotRecorder
98	        )
99	        self.poller = poller
100	        self.snapshotScheduler = snapshotScheduler
101	        self.accountMonitor = accountMonitor
102	        self.codexMonitor = codexMonitor
103	        self.refreshState = refreshState
104	
105	        let usagesPath = fileManager.filePath
106	        let fileWatcher = UsagesFileWatcher(path: usagesPath)
107	        let store = UsageStore(fileWatcher: fileWatcher, preferences: Self.sharedPreferences)
108	        self.usageStore = store
109	        Self.sharedStore = store
110	
111	        setupStatusItem(store: store, refreshState: refreshState, historyReader: historyReader)
112	        trackStoreChanges(store: store)
113	
114	        Task {
115	            await StartupMigrationRunner(fileManager: fileManager).run()
116	            store.start()
117	            await poller.start()
118	            await snapshotScheduler.start()
119	            await accountMonitor.start()
120	            await codexMonitor.start()
121	        }
122	    }
123	
124	    func applicationWillTerminate(_ notification: Notification) {
125	        quit()
126	    }
127	
128	    // MARK: - Status item
129	
130	    private func setupStatusItem(
131	        store: UsageStore,
132	        refreshState: RefreshState,
133	        historyReader: UsageHistoryReader
134	    ) {
135	        let item = NSStatusBar.system.statusItem(withLength: NSStatusItem.variableLength)
136	        statusItem = item
137	
138	        let popover = NSPopover()
139	        popover.behavior = .transient
140	        let hosting = NSHostingController(
141	            rootView: UsageDetailsView(
142	                store: store,
143	                refreshState: refreshState,
144	                historyReader: historyReader,
145	                onRefresh: { [weak self] in
146	                    await self?.poller?.pollOnce(now: Date(), force: true)
147	                },
148	                onOpenSettings: { [weak self] in
149	                    self?.openSettings()
150	                },
151	                onQuit: { [weak self] in
152	                    self?.quit()
153	                }
154	            )
155	        )
156	        // Let SwiftUI's intrinsic size drive the popover so it grows with content
157	        // up to the screen-ratio cap enforced inside UsageDetailsView.
158	        hosting.sizingOptions = [.preferredContentSize]
159	        popover.contentViewController = hosting
160	        self.popover = popover
161	
162	        if let button = item.button {
163	            button.target = self
164	            button.action = #selector(togglePopover(_:))
165	            // The menu bar appearance can change (wallpaper-driven tinting) without
166	            // the app's effectiveAppearance changing, so we observe the button itself.
167	            appearanceObserver = button.observe(\.effectiveAppearance, options: [.new]) { [weak self] _, _ in
168	                Task { @MainActor in self?.refreshStatusItemImage() }
169	            }
170	        }
171	
172	        refreshStatusItemImage()
173	    }
174	
175	    private func refreshStatusItemImage() {
176	        guard let store = usageStore, let button = statusItem?.button else { return }
177	        let isDark = button.effectiveAppearance.bestMatch(from: [.darkAqua, .aqua]) == .darkAqua
178	        let isUnconfigured = Self.sharedPreferences.menuBarSegments.isEmpty
179	        let image = MenuBarLabelRenderer.render(
180	            segments: store.menuBarSegments,
181	            separator: Self.sharedPreferences.menuBarSeparator,
182	            fallbackText: store.menuBarText,
183	            isDarkMenuBar: isDark,
184	            isUnconfigured: isUnconfigured
185	        )
186	        button.image = image
187	    }
188	
189	    @objc private func togglePopover(_ sender: Any?) {
190	        guard let popover, let button = statusItem?.button else { return }
191	        if popover.isShown {
192	            popover.performClose(sender)
193	        } else {
194	            popover.show(relativeTo: button.bounds, of: button, preferredEdge: .minY)
195	            popover.contentViewController?.view.window?.makeKey()
196	        }
197	    }
198	
199	    // MARK: - Observation
200	
201	    /// Re-arms `withObservationTracking` after every change so the status item
202	    /// keeps mirroring the store. Tracking fires exactly once per registration.
203	    private func trackStoreChanges(store: UsageStore) {
204	        withObservationTracking {
205	            _ = store.menuBarSegments
206	            _ = store.menuBarText
207	        } onChange: { [weak self] in
208	            Task { @MainActor in
209	                guard let self, let store = self.usageStore else { return }
210	                self.refreshStatusItemImage()
211	                self.trackStoreChanges(store: store)
212	            }
213	        }
214	    }
215	
216	    // MARK: - Settings
217	
218	    private func openSettings() {
219	        popover?.performClose(nil)
220	        // .accessory apps have no dock presence; showSettingsWindow: silently fails
221	        // unless we temporarily switch to .regular so AppKit can make the window key.
222	        NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.regular)
223	        NSApp.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true)
224	
225	        // SwiftUI creates the Settings window lazily on first showSettingsWindow: —
226	        // if it already exists, reuse it directly to avoid responder-chain races
227	        // that occur during the popover-dismissal + activation-policy transition.
228	        if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
229	            settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
230	        } else {
231	            // First-time path: AppKit needs a runloop tick after the .accessory →
232	            // .regular transition to install the standard App menu (which is where
233	            // SwiftUI wires the Settings… action). sendAction with a nil target
234	            // relies on that wiring, so we trigger the menu item directly once
235	            // AppKit has had a chance to build it.
236	            DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.15) {
237	                Self.triggerSettingsMenuItem()
238	                if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
239	                    settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
240	                }
241	            }
242	        }
243	
244	        // Restore .accessory policy once the settings window is closed.
245	        if settingsWindowObserver == nil {
246	            // Use selector-based observer so the callback runs on @MainActor naturally,
247	            // avoiding Sendable-crossing issues with block-based addObserver(queue:).
248	            settingsWindowObserver = self
249	            NotificationCenter.default.addObserver(
250	                self,
251	                selector: #selector(handleSettingsWindowWillClose(_:)),
252	                name: NSWindow.willCloseNotification,
253	                object: nil
254	            )
255	        }
256	    }

258	    @objc private func handleSettingsWindowWillClose(_ notification: Notification) {
259	        // Only react to the Settings window closing — popovers and other
260	        // transient windows also emit willClose.
261	        guard let closingWindow = notification.object as? NSWindow else { return }
262	        let windowIdentifier = closingWindow.identifier?.rawValue ?? ""
263	        guard windowIdentifier.contains("Settings") || windowIdentifier.contains("Preferences") else { return }
264	        // willClose fires while the window is still visible and before
265	        // AppKit tears it down; defer to the next runloop tick so the
266	        // activation-policy change isn't overridden by pending AppKit work.
267	        DispatchQueue.main.async { [weak self] in
268	            NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.accessory)
269	            // Accessory transition doesn't always remove the app from the
270	            // Cmd+Tab / app-switcher list until another app is activated.
271	            if let frontmost = NSWorkspace.shared.runningApplications.first(where: {
272	                $0.isActive && $0.processIdentifier != ProcessInfo.processInfo.processIdentifier
273	            }) {
274	                frontmost.activate()
275	            } else {
276	                NSApp.hide(nil)
277	            }
278	            guard let self else { return }
279	            NotificationCenter.default.removeObserver(self, name: NSWindow.willCloseNotification, object: nil)
280	            self.settingsWindowObserver = nil
281	        }
282	    }

284	    /// SwiftUI tags its Settings window with an identifier containing "Settings"
285	    /// (or "Preferences" on older SDKs). Match on the stable identifier rather
286	    /// than localized titles.
287	    private static func findSettingsWindow() -> NSWindow? {
288	        NSApp.windows.first { window in
289	            let identifier = window.identifier?.rawValue ?? ""
290	            return identifier.contains("Settings") || identifier.contains("Preferences")
291	        }

294	    /// Walk the main menu looking for the App menu's Settings… (or Preferences…)
295	    /// item and invoke it. Going through the menu guarantees the action is
296	    /// routed to whichever responder SwiftUI wired up, instead of relying on
297	    /// `sendAction(_:to:from:)` to resolve a nil target in the responder chain
298	    /// — which is unreliable right after an activation-policy switch.
299	    private static func triggerSettingsMenuItem() {
300	        guard let mainMenu = NSApp.mainMenu else { return }
301	        for topItem in mainMenu.items {
302	            guard let submenu = topItem.submenu else { continue }
303	            for (index, item) in submenu.items.enumerated() {
304	                let title = item.title
305	                if title.hasPrefix("Settings") || title.hasPrefix("Preferences") {
306	                    submenu.performActionForItem(at: index)
307	                    return
308	                }
309	            }
310	        }
311	    }

4	/// Rasterises the full menu bar label into a non-template `NSImage`.
5	/// MenuBarExtra's label was unusable: template tinting stripped the per-tier
6	/// dot colors, and mixed HStacks silently truncated segments past the first.
7	/// With a custom `NSStatusItem`, we control `isTemplate = false` so hues
8	/// survive and we pick the text color from the status button's effective
9	/// appearance (which tracks the menu bar, not the app).
10	@MainActor
11	enum MenuBarLabelRenderer {
12	    private static let font: NSFont = .menuBarFont(ofSize: 0)
13	    private static let dot = "● "
14	    private static let horizontalPadding: CGFloat = 4
15	    private static let height: CGFloat = 18
16	    static let unconfiguredCallToAction = "Configure AI Metrics"
17	
18	    static func render(
19	        segments: [MenuBarSegment],
20	        separator: String,
21	        fallbackText: String,
22	        isDarkMenuBar: Bool,
23	        isUnconfigured: Bool = false
24	    ) -> NSImage {
25	        let textColor: NSColor = isDarkMenuBar ? .white : .black
26	        let attributed: NSAttributedString
27	        if isUnconfigured && segments.isEmpty {
28	            attributed = unconfiguredAttributedString(textColor: textColor)
29	        } else {
30	            attributed = attributedString(
31	                segments: segments,
32	                separator: separator,
33	                fallbackText: fallbackText,
34	                textColor: textColor
35	            )
36	        }
37	        let textSize = attributed.size()
38	        let width = ceil(textSize.width) + horizontalPadding * 2
39	        let size = NSSize(width: max(width, 1), height: height)
40	        let image = NSImage(size: size)
41	        image.lockFocus()
42	        let textY = (height - ceil(textSize.height)) / 2
43	        attributed.draw(at: NSPoint(x: horizontalPadding, y: textY))
44	        image.unlockFocus()
45	        image.isTemplate = false
46	        return image
47	    }
48	
49	    private static func unconfiguredAttributedString(textColor: NSColor) -> NSAttributedString {
50	        let result = NSMutableAttributedString()
51	        let gear = NSImage(systemSymbolName: "gearshape", accessibilityDescription: nil)
52	        if let gear {
53	            let configured = gear.withSymbolConfiguration(
54	                NSImage.SymbolConfiguration(pointSize: font.pointSize, weight: .regular)
55	            ) ?? gear
56	            configured.isTemplate = true
57	            let tinted = NSImage(size: configured.size, flipped: false) { rect in
58	                configured.draw(in: rect)
59	                textColor.set()
60	                rect.fill(using: .sourceAtop)
61	                return true
62	            }
63	            tinted.isTemplate = false
64	            let attachment = NSTextAttachment()
65	            attachment.image = tinted
66	            attachment.bounds = CGRect(
67	                x: 0,
68	                y: -1,
69	                width: tinted.size.width,
70	                height: tinted.size.height
71	            )
72	            result.append(NSAttributedString(attachment: attachment))
73	            result.append(NSAttributedString(
74	                string: " ",
75	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
76	            ))
77	        }
78	        result.append(NSAttributedString(
79	            string: unconfiguredCallToAction,
80	            attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
81	        ))
82	        return result
83	    }
84	
85	    private static func attributedString(
86	        segments: [MenuBarSegment],
87	        separator: String,
88	        fallbackText: String,
89	        textColor: NSColor
90	    ) -> NSAttributedString {
91	        guard !segments.isEmpty else {
92	            return NSAttributedString(
93	                string: fallbackText,
94	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
95	            )
96	        }
97	        let result = NSMutableAttributedString()
98	        for (index, segment) in segments.enumerated() {
99	            if index > 0, !separator.isEmpty {
100	                result.append(NSAttributedString(
101	                    string: separator,
102	                    attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
103	                ))
104	            }
105	            if let vendor = segment.vendorIcon,
106	               let icon = VendorBranding.tintedNSImage(for: vendor, height: font.capHeight) {
107	                let attachment = NSTextAttachment()
108	                attachment.image = icon
109	                let pad = VendorBranding.iconGlowPadding
110	                attachment.bounds = CGRect(
111	                    x: -pad,
112	                    y: -pad,
113	                    width: icon.size.width,
114	                    height: icon.size.height
115	                )
116	                result.append(NSAttributedString(attachment: attachment))
117	                result.append(NSAttributedString(
118	                    string: "\u{2009}",
119	                    attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
120	                ))
121	            }
122	            if segment.showDot {
123	                let dotColor = segment.tier?.nsColor ?? textColor
124	                result.append(NSAttributedString(
125	                    string: dot,
126	                    attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: dotColor]
127	                ))
128	            }
129	            result.append(NSAttributedString(
130	                string: segment.text,
131	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
132	            ))
133	        }
134	        return result
135	    }
136	}

content.text:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

preferences.menuBarSegments[index] in its closure. During a SwiftUI state
transaction for segment deletion, the binding's get was re-evaluated after
removeAll(_:) shrunk the array but before the card view was removed from
the tree — causing Array._checkSubscript to trap.

Fixed by having SegmentCardView resolve the segment by id rather than
capturing index. The binding now re-resolves the id in its getter/setter
via firstIndex(where:), making it immune to concurrent array mutations.

3c2d4d1 fix(launch-at-login): unify state check and make registration idempotent
Repeated setEnabled(true) calls from swift run were stacking duplicate legacy
login items because SMAppService.mainApp.register() — when called from a
non-bundled binary — has no idempotence and appends rather than merges.

Now isEnabled checks both BTM (modern SMAppService entry) and legacy System
Events login items, so the idempotence guard reliably detects the true state
whether running from .app bundle or swift run. Legacy items are queried via
AppleScript (requires Automation permission, gracefully falls back if denied).

Also add NSAppleEventsUsageDescription to Info.plist so macOS permits the
Automation query.

integer to ensure windows always slide forward with time. Each 60s tick
now recalculates referenceDate = Date() - (offset × interval), making
navigation-history windows glide naturally. Offset resets to 0 on window
size changes.
